data.title: Community Healthcare Trust: High Yield, No Growth, Limited Cushion
data.description:
At first glance, Community Healthcare Trust (CHCT) looks compelling enough with its ~11.6% yield and ~12.9x EV/EBITDA multiple versus ~16.7x for the sector. Such numbers usually mean the REIT is being mispriced.

If you look at the fundamentals, though, it tells a different story. There has been very little AFFO growth, with just ~0.5% 5-year CAGR. Payout ratios are high at ~88-95%, leaving no margin of error. Plus an unresolved situation with a tenant that we will discuss shortly adds to cash flow uncertainty.

So the market is pricing in real risks here, not undervaluing CHCT. This supports a Hold at best, and I am biased towards downside if I see no improvement in execution.

CHCT Has A Functional Model, But Not Premium Real Estate

CHCT runs a diversified healthcare real estate portfolio. They focus on community-based facilities like medical office buildings, behavioral facilities, rehab centers, and specialty clinics. As of Q4 2025, the company owns 198 properties with ~$1.2B in gross investments. The occupancy rate is ~90.6% with a ~7-year weighted average lease term (or WALT).

CHCT mostly buys in non-urban markets and leases them locally. However, the model comes with its own trade-offs. First, their tenants are generally smaller operators, not large health systems, so while occupancy may be high, there is the risk of default. Second, many assets are in non-core areas, so pricing power is limited. Third, as a result of both of the two above, tenants are difficult to replace, so the portfolio performance becomes tied to operators, not the asset itself.

We can see the effects of these in execution. In the current setup—decent but not tight occupancy—how well specific tenants do drives the durability of CHCT’s cash flow.

This does not mean operations are weak, however. There is decent leasing activity with renewals and new leases. WALT went up from 6.7 to 7.0 years. CHCT sells old properties, recently at around 7.9% cap rates. Their new properties came with ~9.1–9.7% expected returns. So the platform seems to be functioning, management seems to be disciplined, and not just reacting to distress.

Operating Trends — Stable Surface, Underlying Fragility

CHCT has seen occupancy improving a little. New leases have not declined. Overall, the operating metrics seem fine. Some of these are:

  • Revenue growth — ~5.6% year-over-year to ~$30.9M,

  • FFO — increased to $0.49/share (+4.6% YoY), and

  • AFFO — remained steady at $0.55/share.

CHCT should also be adding to its rent going into 2026 from redevelopments and new acquisitions. All of these give the appearance of a stable, functioning operating base.

However, there are real issues beneath the surface.

One immediate issue is with a behavioral hospital operator who rents six properties. CHCT was only able to collect ~$200K of rent from this tenant during the quarter. The tenant signed a LOI in July 2025 to sell all six hospital operations to a new operator. However, this has not been completed yet, and management is unsure whether it will close. To add to the problem, the deal is structured as a single transaction across all six properties, so the risk is concentrated – and real.

Therefore, physical occupancy may appear stable, but cash flow from certain assets may be in impaired territory, or uncertain. Since CHCT does not have a lot of margin to absorb these disruptions, this can directly pressure AFFO.

Financials — Growth Has Flatlined

The lack of growth we have outlined is not just one incident. There is a real lack of growth over many years. So, for example, in the last 3 years, their AFFO growth was actually negative at ~(4.8)%. The five-year number is hardly any better at ~0.5%, and FWD is ~0.3%. Their operating cash flow was only ~$56M from operations, well below sector levels and declining YoY.

Even their most recent quarter's growth has been largely appearance – driven mostly by acquisitions and capital recycling. AFFO still remained flat YoY and declined slightly QoQ. The business is not compounding, only maintaining earnings through portfolio rotation.

This creates hurdles in their ability to grow dividends. They do not have the balance sheet flexibility to acquire new properties without recycling old ones. Therefore, there is no clear path to multiple expansion.

Dividend — High Yield, Tight Coverage

CHCT’s dividend is high – a key reason for investor interest. But it is also the biggest source of risk. Current yield is ~11.6–11.7%, and annual payout is ~$1.91. This is attractive relative to the REIT sector. However, if you look at the coverage, that’s a different story altogether:

AFFO payout ratio: ~88–90%Cash payout ratio: ~95%

That leaves no room for error. If there’s tenant disruption, or any increase in interest expense, which on the whole causes temporary AFFO declines – CHCT has no fallback option. Recent dividend growth has slowed to ~0.5–1% annually, versus ~2+% historically.

The underlying growth is weak, which is reflected in these numbers. The company has a long track record of quarterly dividend increases since IPO. Recently, this has become nominal, with just ~$0.0025 per quarter increase lately. The dividend is really a constraint on the business and does not look sustainable – which results in an F rating on Seeking Alpha.

Balance Sheet — Limited Flexibility

The balance sheet is also troubling. Debt/equity ratio was ~125%, while interest coverage was ~1.1%. So leverage is high, and they only generate a little more operating income than their interest expense, so that leaves very little margin if earnings weaken or rates rise.

Currently, total debt consists of a $400M revolving credit facility and ~$275M in term loans ($125M due 2028 and $150M due 2030). That means total committed debt capacity is north of ~$675M, with roughly $533M–$536M currently outstanding. Debt to gross real estate asset looks reasonable at ~43-44%, but with weak earnings growth, this gives them no capacity to take on more debt without pressuring cash flow. Admittedly, these are not distressed levels, but they are tight for a REIT with weak growth coupled with high payout ratios.

Given what we discussed now, we can see that CHCT remains sensitive to refinancing. Their operating income hardly covers their interest expenses. That means, if rates go up or refinancing expenses rise, that will put pressure on AFFO.

They are already paying ~$7M per quarter on interest expense — that is nearly 47% of their $14.9M quarterly AFFO, which is calculated after interest expense. ~$13M is needed per quarter for dividends, leaving very little cash in hand. So the only way CHCT can grow without dilution is by recycling capital.

For example, recently, CHCT sold a property at a ~7.9% cap rate. New investments (~$64.5M in 2025 and ~$122.5M pipeline) are getting funded without fresh equity.

In summary, the balance sheet is stable, but constrains growth. It does not break the story, but it doesn’t make it either.

Valuation — Cheap, But Not Mispriced

CHCT looks inexpensive from many aspects. Its EV/EBITDA is lower than the sector, and its price/book (~1.03x vs ~1.53x) and price/cash flow (~7.8x vs ~12.6x) are both lower than the sector. The dividend is also well above peers.

At first glance, CHCT looks like a classic value deal, with discounted multiples and high income. However, with the AFFO at ~$0.55 per quarter, annualized to ~$2.20, the ~$1.91 dividend starts looking disproportionate. Given the negligible forward AFFO growth, there is very little room left for either dividend growth or multiple expansion.

Speaking of multiple expansion, if we were to want to rerate CHCT from ~12.9x EV/EBITDA to the sector ~16–17x, that would require strong AFFO growth or risk reduction. Such a rerating would imply a ~30% upside (16.7 ÷ 12.9 − 1). But with AFFO growth near zero and tenant risk unresolved, there is no clear catalyst to support that expansion.

In other words, the market seems to be pricing CHCT’s risks appropriately.

Conclusion — Hold, With A Negative Bias

CHCT at first glance looks attractive, with high dividend yield and discounted pricing. However, the underlying fundamentals make it less than appealing. AFFO has been almost flat for many years, the dividend is not well covered, and the balance sheet is not flexible enough. All these are not separate problems — they are the manifestations of the same problem, a stagnant business. Then we have tenant risks. The valuation, therefore, is not an opportunity — it should rather be read as a warning.

For all these reasons, I consider CHCT a Hold, but not one I would add to. For strictly income-focused investors, the yield may justify holding existing positions. However, without clear growth improvement or tenant stability, adding at this time would be risky.

data.title: Rates Spark: Continue To Play The End-Of-War Game
data.description:
By Padhraic Garvey, CFA, Regional Head of Research, Americas; Benjamin Schroeder, Senior Rates Strategist; and Michiel Tukker, Senior UK & Eurozone Rates Strategist

Base case, we expect to see a way through this whole mess

It's painfully slow, but we see enough out of the Iran ceasefire narrative to extrapolate an outcome that culminates in an end to these particular hostilities. As noted, this likely involves the US taking control of Iranian uranium enrichment while Iran retains some semblance of control over the Strait (even if in an acknowledged 'monitoring' role).

As it is, we're in something of a vacuum, as the official negotiations don't start until Saturday. But with Israel now reaching out to Lebanon, it's clear that the US is paving the way for a final outcome. The fact that the strait remains broadly closed misses the bigger point.

As a stand-alone issue, this is, of course, not good. But this is not the end game. It's Iran pre-positioning ahead of talks. Base case: we expect to see a way through this whole mess.

The market reaction maintains a positive gloss overall. For US Treasuries, the curve is beginning to show a tendency to re-steepen. The path for the front end is lower yields, as there is a price to be paid for the inflation angst now and ahead, which is ultimately negative for the economy.

Meanwhile, longer-dated yields should be curbed from moving too much lower on account of the inflation risks ahead. The 5yr area of the curve continues to re-richen, which makes sense as the market nods towards Fed rate cuts to come later in the year.

All the while, there is room for the curve to steepen from both ends. Thursday's 30yr auction struggled, while Tuesday's 3yr auction did better, fitting with the aforementioned steeper curve bias.

Friday sees the March CPI reading, and we are set to see a comfortable 3% handle on headline inflation. This is a clear repercussion of the higher oil and energy prices and will remain a feature in the coming months as the impact of the higher oil price continues to feed through.

Troublingly, core inflation (which excludes energy) will rise too, even if in a more moderate manner. The market should be expecting most of this. But even still, confirmation of higher inflation reminds us that it is a thing, and will remain so in the coming number of months. Market break-even inflation rates in fact anticipate a rise in US inflation towards 4%. A first step in that direction will be confirmed by Friday's inflation report.

Rebound from the initial spread tightening in EGB space has been rather muted

Oil prices remain more sensitive to lingering tensions and continue to gradually creep higher again. The feed-through to front-end rates via inflation and central bank hiking expectations remains more direct. EUR rates markets are again eyeing the possibility of the ECB hiking more than the 50bp, which has apparently become the baseline for this year.

Amid rates creeping higher, one could also be interpreted as a sign of caution, given that the Finnish sale of a new 10y benchmark attracted an order book only around half of the usual size for its past 10y deals.

However, beyond those indications of returning caution, overall market risk sentiment appears more ready to look through the crisis by now. Despite all indications that the ceasefire is a fragile one, this growing optimism perhaps rests more on the notion sinking in that US President Trump needs to appease a domestic audience, limiting his appetite to escalate further.

We observe that equities have not given up the gains on the initial positive news as readily, with the VIX index actually staying close to its lowest levels since early March. And even in rates markets, we have witnessed a bear steepening. This bear steepening has gelled with a tendency of longer real rates also wanting to rise again, at least on the EUR side.

Also, as a sign of returning optimism, the rebound from the initial spread tightening in the EGB space has been rather muted. The 10y BTP/Bund was only modestly wider on the day. And more indicative of the markets pricing out severe tail risks was that Bunds have lost more ground versus swaps.

Friday's events and market view

The highlight will be US CPI data, whereby markets are bracing for an increase from 2.4% to 3.4% for the headline number, while core is expected to rise mildly from 2.5% to 2.7%. In terms of issuance, we have Italy with a 3Y, 7Y and 15Y BTP auction for a total of €8bn.

Content Disclaimer

This publication has been prepared by ING solely for information purposes irrespective of a particular user's means, financial situation or investment objectives. The information does not constitute investment recommendation, and nor is it investment, legal or tax advice or an offer or solicitation to purchase or sell any financial instrument. Read more

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: QLC: Quality And Value Tilt, Strong Track Record (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
The FlexShares US Quality Large Cap Index ETF (QLC) follows a multi-factor investment approach with a clear bias toward profitability and disciplined valuation. While its sector exposure has been close to a neutral sector mix since my last review of this fund nearly one year ago, its stock selection has also remained anchored by players, such as big tech and large financial institutions. In the context of a mixed but still constructive macroeconomic environment, QLC has gained increased appeal for investors looking for selective exposure to the stock market. The coming sections will detail the fund’s core holdings and compare its sector mix, valuation, and performance with the broader market and other multi-factor ETFs.

ETF Description & Highlights

QLC is designed to offer exposure to U.S. companies with a combination of better-than-average quality, value, and momentum characteristics. The fund follows a rules-based methodology, tracking the Northern Trust Quality Large Cap Index, which employs an investment process that starts with an initial universe of the 600 largest U.S. companies. From there, each company is measured by a multi-factor model that scores them based on quality metrics (profitability and balance sheet strength), valuation indicators, including their value relative to historical averages and peers, and price momentum. Then, stocks are selected and weighted in the index through an optimization phase that aims to maximize the multi-factor exposure while limiting risk based on individual stocks and sector weights, as well as beta and turnover constraints. The index is rebalanced on a quarterly basis to reflect current market conditions.

As of March 27, 2026, QLC had $775 million in assets under management, invested in a portfolio of 175 companies, with an average market capitalization of $316.2 billion. The fund remains concentrated in the upper end of the U.S. equity market, with mega caps accounting for 40.5% of total assets, large caps 30.7%, and mid caps 28.1%, representing only a small shift from the picture a year ago, as the allocation to mega caps edged down from 41.2% to 40.5%, while mid caps increased from 26.5% to 28.1%. This allocation profile is broadly similar to the Russell 1000 index, a large- and mid-cap equity benchmark, tracked by the iShares Russell 1000 ETF (IWB), though a bit more skewed toward mid caps, as this category accounts for only 20.2% of the index, compared to QLC’s 28.1%.

QLC’s top ten holdings include NVIDIA (NVDA) (7.08%), Apple (AAPL) (7.05%), Alphabet (GOOGL) (Class A at 3.94% and Class C at 3.54%), Microsoft (MSFT) (3.88%), Broadcom (AVGO) (2.70%), Amazon (AMZN) (2.19%), Berkshire Hathaway (BRK.B) (2.07%), Johnson & Johnson (JNJ) (2.02%), and JPMorgan (JPM) (1.89%), with a heavy concentration in big tech names, mirroring, to a large extent, the top holdings of broader equity indexes

The following table presents a mix of U.S. factor-oriented ETFs that will serve as the peer group for QLC in this analysis. The first is the iShares U.S. Equity Factor ETF (LRGF), which tracks the MSCI USA Diversified Multiple-Factor Index with a modified market-cap weighting approach. Next, the SPDR MSCI USA StrategicFactors ETF (QUS) incorporates low volatility alongside value, momentum, and quality measures in the investment decision process. The Franklin U.S. Large Cap Multifactor Index ETF (FLQL) tracks the LibertyQ U.S. Large Cap Equity Index, using an optimization process to optimize exposure to multiple factors while controlling for risk and turnover. John Hancock Multifactor Large Cap ETF (JHML) relies on a proprietary multifactor model that weights stocks based on characteristics such as profitability, valuation, and momentum. Meanwhile, the Vident U.S. Equity Strategy ETF (VUSE) follows the Vident Core U.S. Equity Index, employing a market-cap-weighted strategy that results in broad exposure across all market sizes. Lastly, the Pacer Lunt Large Cap Multi-Factor Alternator ETF (PALC) takes a more tactical approach, rotating among factor exposures based on momentum measures, which adds a dynamic element to the allocation process.

QLC’s largest allocation is by far to technology (33.4%), followed by a second tier composed of financial services (13.8%), communication services (12.3%), and healthcare (11.4%). A more moderate allocation is spread across consumer discretionary (7.9%) and industrials (6.8%), while the remaining exposure includes utilities (3.8%), consumer staples (3.5%), energy (2.4%), basic materials (2.4%), and real estate (2.4%). This sector mix results in overweight positions in communication services (+2.3%), healthcare (+1.7%), and technology (+1.4%), along with underweight exposure to industrials (-2.6%), consumer discretionary (-2.4%), and energy (-1.8%). As we can see, although not formally expressed in its summary prospectus, in practice, the fund seems to target a neutral sector exposure, as sector allocation gaps relative to the Russell 1000 remain in the 2.5% range. Therefore, it is not surprising that the fund sector allocation profile has changed little since early 2025, with only minor allocation adjustments since then.

Breaking down QLC’s allocation across its largest sectors, we see that in information technology, the biggest sleeve is in semiconductors, led by holdings in NVIDIA (NVDA), Broadcom Inc. (AVGO), and KLA Corp. (KLAC), which position the fund at the center of AI and data infrastructure but also leave its sector performance heavily tied to hyperscaler demand. Another large position is Apple Inc. (AAPL), adding exposure to consumer demand, while a meaningful position in Microsoft Corp. (MSFT) reinforces a quality-growth tilt. These core positions, along with holdings in Cisco Systems (CSCO) and Jabil Inc. (JBL), give the fund a quality profile, where upside is likely to be driven by sentiment toward a few large players rather than by broad-based sector performance.

In financial services, the main exposure is through diversified banks, led by JPMorgan Chase & Co. (JPM), Citigroup Inc. (C), Bank of America Corporation (BAC), and Wells Fargo (WFC), which signals a preference for scale and suggests the fund is leaning into institutions with financial strength to navigate credit cycles rather than smaller and more rate-sensitive institutions. The fund also holds positions in Goldman Sachs Group (GS), Morgan Stanley (MS), and insurance players such as American International Group (AIG) and Aflac Incorporated (AFL), which add a defensive income component within the sector. Altogether, it feels like a disciplined sector positioning: broad in terms of industry exposure but still selective so that it can fit a quality mandate, favoring large companies with systemic relevance that are likely more resilient in the event of an economic downturn.

Holdings in communication services are more concentrated, with only a few positions, including overweight exposure to Alphabet Inc. (GOOGL) and Meta Platforms (META), turning the sector into a high-conviction bet on these two giants and highlighting the fund's focus on large and profitable companies rather than a market-cap-weighted allocation to the sector. The flip side, though, is an increased reliance on company-specific issues such as advertising and platform monetization trends, as well as regulatory pressures.

Beyond the core sectors, QLC maintains smaller allocations that round out the portfolio but do not materially alter the fund’s tilt toward large players. The largest holdings in healthcare are prominent pharmaceutical companies such as Johnson & Johnson (JNJ) and Bristol-Myers Squibb (BMY), while the fund holds aerospace and defense names in industrials, including GE Aerospace (GE) and Lockheed Martin Corp. (LMT). Overall, what we can see across sectors is a consistent pattern: this is an ETF that seeks a selective allocation to market leaders, indicating a tilt toward companies with competitive positioning and strong market scale. The downside is a reliance on a relatively narrow group of stocks that may lead to performance dislocations from broader market indexes from time to time. Of course, this may be a good thing in an equity market with a small group of outperformers, as has been the case for much of the past few years, with narrow leadership, but it may turn out to be a headwind if market leadership broadens, as many expect once the macroeconomic backdrop normalizes.

While QLC shows to a large extent a neutral sector allocation relative to broader equity indexes, the peer group of multifactor ETFs takes a more conservative stance, on average. Funds like QUS and JHML have nearly 25% allocated to technology, for instance. In addition, PALC, VUSE, and QUS favor consumer staples, a defensive sector by nature. Still, we can arguably say that the sector correlation among peers is fairly high at over 0.89 relative to QLC, which suggests a common view in maintaining an allocation profile somewhat close to market-cap equity indexes like the Russell 1000.

Reasonable Valuation

QLC’s 18.5x P/E represents roughly a 15% discount to the Russell 1000’s 21.8x. That discount is broad-based, with cheaper valuations in several core sectors, including financial services, consumer staples, and industrials, where valuations are over 30% lower than in the index.

The fund shows slightly stronger growth measures than the Russell 1000. Its historical earnings growth of 10.9% surpasses the index’s 8.7%, while sales growth of 8.4% also comes in above the benchmark’s 7.0%. The key driver is tied to the stock selection in sectors like technology, communication services, and healthcare, as the fund overweights high-growth names in these sectors, like NVIDIA, Alphabet Inc., and ResMed Inc. (RMD). At the same time, QLC’s profitability metrics compare well with the Russell 1000. The fund’s 35.6% EBITDA margin is 1.4 percentage points higher than the benchmark’s 34.2%, while its 45.9% ROE outpaces the index by 4.7 points, driven by holdings in technology, healthcare, and consumer staples that are significantly higher than in the Russell 1000.

Overall, QLC looks like a fund where its multi-factor design helps keep valuation from getting stretched while still maintaining relatively higher profitability than the benchmark, given a selective stock-picking and weighting process. Looking ahead, in light of its balanced approach, I do not expect meaningful changes in the sector mix. Rather, core holdings in its largest sectors, like technology, communications services, and financials, should continue to anchor the fund’s above-average profitability metrics. Of course, drastic changes in market leadership may lead to adjustments in the portfolio, influenced by the momentum component of the fund’s methodology, but it is not the most likely scenario, as the underperformance of a group of stocks would make them more attractive from a valuation perspective, which would also play a role in the overall multi-factor investment process. In summary, this dynamic should keep the fund’s style tilted toward quality with relatively reasonable valuation, even if the market leadership rotates a bit away from some big tech names from here.

The peer group of multi-factor ETFs also trades at a discount to the Russell 1000, with most of them near a 20x P/E level. This is consistent with the more conservative sector mix in general, driven by the effect of factors like value and low volatility on their portfolio’s composition. Still, QLC’s valuation is a bit lower than peers, which seems to reflect the fund’s disciplined stock selection, with limited or no exposure to high-multiple names like Boeing (BA), Tesla Inc. (TSLA), Costco Wholesale Corp. (COST), and Palantir Technologies Inc. (PLTR), for instance.

Outperforming The Broader Market

With annualized returns of 21.7% over the 3-year period and 13.5% over the 5-year period, QLC has delivered stronger results than the Russell 1000. QLC has also outpaced the peer group, with the best performance over those same windows. More recently, that relative strength has become even more clear, with QLC ahead of both the benchmark and peers by a large margin over the 1-year period. The biggest relative weak spots were 2020, when it lagged the Russell 1000 by 7.1%, and 2022, when it trailed the peer group during that negative year for stocks. Still, those years were more than offset by strong returns in 2024 and 2025.

On the volatility side, QLC has behaved much more like the broad market than a defensive strategy. Its beta has been in the 0.99-1.00 range, just below IWB. A similar pattern is seen in peers like FLQL, JHML, and PALC, whose betas have also been slightly lower than the benchmark. Meanwhile, funds QUS and VUSE have maintained betas below 0.95, pointing to a prevalent role played by the low-volatility factor. That said, we have seen most peers exhibiting more contained price action in both directions, as illustrated by their capture ratios below 100 over time for most funds. That is not the case for QLC, though, whose capture ratios have been a bit inconsistent over time. Still, QLC’s outperformance in the 3-year period is reflected in a strong participation during rallies, with an upside capture ratio of 105, while prices declined less than the benchmark during selloffs, as indicated by its downside capture ratio of 94. Overall, although QLC’s returns have been positive over time, the fund has seen over the past three years a strong outperformance both in bullish periods and in more risk-off environments.

With an average daily volume of $5.02 million, QLC’s liquidity is solid, though it still trails larger peers like LRGF. Despite that, its bid-ask spread of 0.13% is wider than the peer group average of 0.10%. The fund’s 19% turnover is low and broadly consistent with several passive peers, especially compared with peers like FLQL, VUSE, and particularly PALC, which show much higher trading activity. This helps support an expense ratio of 0.25%, though still above cheaper funds in the category like LRGF, QUS, and FLQL. QLC offers a 0.93% dividend yield, which is slightly below the peer group and the broader market, making it less appealing for income-oriented investors, though the difference is not significant. The fund’s tracking error of roughly 0.5% is one of the best in the group, while its Sharpe ratio of 1.55 (3-year) and 0.83 (5-year) is the best among peers, indicating that its strong returns have more than compensated for its above-average volatility.

Upside Drivers And Risk Considerations

Despite current uncertainties, the current economic backdrop remains generally positive, with recent data pointing to manufacturing still expanding in 2026 and S&P 500 earnings projected to grow at a healthy pace. That said, QLC’s quality bias and valuation discipline may continue to work if the market remains selective. The fund’s discount to the Russell 1000, combined with above-benchmark profitability, gives it a reasonable case for upside if leadership remains more concentrated in high-quality large caps or broadens only gradually.

The catch is that QLC is not a deeply defensive fund, even if its quality tilt could make it look like that at first glance. In the context of current macro overhangs, such as trade policy tensions and energy-driven inflation risk, volatility may stay elevated for a while, and the fund may not hold up as well as peers like QUS and VUSE. In addition, as the fund has a large exposure to a narrow group of big tech names, any sharper rotation away from those stocks could weigh on relative performance. And it would not be unlikely to happen, especially if the optimism around AI infrastructure and hyperscaler spending cools more sharply than expected.

Final Thoughts

Blending quality, valuation, and momentum factors, QLC’s investment strategy has worked over time, delivering a performance that has surpassed the broader market and peers. In addition, above-average profitability and reasonable valuation make the fund an attractive option for investors seeking selective exposure to the equity market, and support my positive view on this fund for the time being. That said, I believe that double-digit upside is realistic for QLC over the next twelve months, especially after the recent pullback, assuming the S&P 500’s earnings growth forecasts remain anchored at current levels.

data.title: EDEN: No Longer Expensive, But Not Much Incentive To Turn Bullish Either
data.description:
A Damp Performance Since Our Previous Coverage

Back in April 2023, I had initiated a hold-rated coverage on the iShares MSCI Denmark ETF (EDEN), a ~$200M-sized passively managed ETF that covers 40 Danish stocks (55% of which consists of giant and large caps). EDEN, which tracks the MSCI Denmark IMI 25/50 Index and is the only ETF in the market that offers pure-play exposure to Danish stocks, hasn’t quite managed to set the world alight since our publication; to elaborate, during an era where other European stocks have generated returns of close to 50% and global stocks have fared even better, EDEN has barely moved, generating just mid-single-digit returns. In fact, take away EDEN’s semi-annual distributions over the past 3 years (which currently annualize to over 3%), and you’ll see that the price performance alone has been quite underwhelming.

Nonetheless, given that it’s been over 3 years since I last assessed this product, I thought it would be a good time to ascertain the investment case again and see if it has been enhanced or worsened.

Political Overhang Is Not Ideal for Reform Momentum

Firstly, investors should note that the political environment in Denmark is quite ambiguous, and the lack of clear leadership could serve as an overhang of sorts on EDEN and Danish stocks. To elaborate, in last month’s general elections, the ruling left-wing party (Social Democratic Party) that had been in power since 2019 took quite a beating (its worst showing in over 120 years), with its coalition falling short of procuring the minimum 90 seats that were needed to secure a majority in Parliament. This prompted the Prime Minister- Mette Frederiksen, to submit her resignation, and, while she could still return for a third term, a lot will depend on the current foreign affairs minister, Lars Løkke Rasmussen, the leader of the Moderate party, who is set to play the role of kingmaker.

If the ruling government doesn’t have the clout to push through reforms to address structural challenges like the housing burden, I don’t see how Danish households are going to feel more confident and emboldened to spend. Investors may be troubled to note that consumer confidence in Denmark has been firmly wedged in the negative territory since 2021!

For added perspective, note that relative to other OECD (Organization for Economic Co-operation and Development) nations, where mortgage and rental costs appear to be well controlled, in Denmark it is quite troubling, with 40% of disposable income going towards meeting these obligations, these housing costs. These pressures are more keenly felt in large cities and urban areas, thus serving as a stumbling block for migration and increased urbanization.

Valuations No Longer Look Expensive, But Improved Dividend Position May Not Last

Back in 2023, one of the primary reasons why I wasn’t eager to pursue EDEN was because its valuations came across as an outlier, not only against its Nordic peers, but also against the broader European region. To expand on this, EDEN was then priced at almost 20x earnings, a 45% premium over its peer set that was priced at an average multiple of less than 14x! Now, though, the valuation situation is quite acceptable, with EDEN’s multiple contracting to just 14.5x, which translates to a marginal discount over the peer set average of 15.1x.

Likewise, EDEN’s income profile also comes across as a little more appealing now. Back then, this ETF was yielding a figure of over 1.3%; now, it is at over 3% and is marginally better than the peer set.

Having said that, EDEN’s dividend payouts in the current year may not necessarily be too dazzling (this could potentially even decline or just stay flat), given the prospects of its top two holdings- Novo Nordisk A/S (NVO) and DSV A/S (DSDVY), both of which jointly account for 30% of the entire portfolio.

NVO (the top holding with an 18% share alone), which is expected to face challenges linked to the loss of exclusivity of Semaglutide in key international markets, is likely to distribute a lower implied figure of DKK 45B towards dividends this year (whereas last year, dividend distributions amounted to DKK 52B). Admittedly, lower distributions are also dictated by a higher share buyback program of DKK 15B (they only spent DKK 1.4B last year), but investors should still be aware that EDEN’s expected FCF of DKK 40B this year is still falling short of covering their total capital return war chest (of DKK 60B) by around DKK 15B. Could this help explain why NVO’s dividend safety grade on Seeking Alpha is quite troubling at D?

With regard to DSDVY, the company’s capital allocation priorities are likely to be primarily focused on using excess cash to bring down its financial gearing, which is beyond their comfort level (currently at 2.8x) following the Schenker acquisition.

Technicals—Risk-Reward Is Better, But No Major Incentive to Pursue EDEN Now

While the risk-reward equation on EDEN’s charts doesn't look too troubling now, it is questionable if investors should be urged to lap up EDEN now.

The image above shows EDEN’s weekly movements over the last 5 years or so, and connecting the peaks and troughs, we have something akin to an ascending channel. Given that the price is closer to the lower boundary of the channel than the upper boundary, it points to a decent risk-reward balance for those thinking of going long.

Having said that, take a look at EDEN’s relative strength [RS] chart, which showcases how it is positioned versus a broader European basket. As things stand, the current RS is now in line with its long-term mean, reducing the incentive to either turn bullish or bearish.

Finally, also consider that this counter is not exactly seeing any meaningful bullish conditions, with the ETF trading below all its key moving averages of 50, 100, and 200 DMA. Going forward, in an environment where risk and high-beta assets are picking up steam post the recent ceasefire, it is questionable if a product like EDEN, which largely revolves around defensive, old-school sectors, will dazzle.

Closing Thoughts

EDEN may no longer be as expensive, but the political overhang in Denmark, weak consumer confidence, a sub-par capital return outlook of EDEN’s top holdings, and a lack of momentum on EDEN’s charts suggest that a long position now would not be ideal. EDEN’s relative strength ratio against other European stocks is also on par with its long-term mean, and suggests that neutral positioning may be more appropriate. Thus, we are reiterating our hold on the EDEN ETF.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: O-I Glass: Progress For 2026 Is Wiped Out Already
data.description:
Shares of O-I Glass (OI) have fallen by a third since mid-February, driven by the conflict in the Middle East, with rising energy prices causing a real overhang here. In fact, this has already been recognized by the business at the very start of the conflict, making the profit pressure incurred likely more severe than initially thought.

This uncertainty, high leverage, and adjusted earnings make me very cautious here. Consequently, I am very cautious about getting involved with the shares or buying the dip here.

Such a conclusion is similar to the one drawn last year by me, with O-I thereby lagging markets at large a great deal, despite all the good intentions, as good intentions and small green shoots, keep getting offset by disappointments elsewhere.

Other, higher conviction ideas, including recent M&A efforts, can be found at Value In Corporate Events.

Coming Under Pressure

O-I presented itself at the Bank of America 2026 Global Agriculture & Materials Conference on the 25th of February. In that presentation, the company maintained the full-year guidance, yet it expects additional pressure on first-quarter earnings per share.

The company sees first-quarter earnings contribution below the previously guided 12-16% full-year contribution. This is largely the result of pressures in Europe, attributable to soft demand, heightened competitive pressures, and additional supply chain costs relating to the closing of excess capacity.

Shares fell to the $13 mark that day and ever since have come down to just $10 per share, driven by the conflict in the Middle East continuing here. With O-I Glass being an energy-intensive business and consumers seeing real pressure on discretionary spending amidst high energy prices, the outlook has realistically only worsened.

A Damp In The Recovery

Note that the implicit profit warning issued towards the end of February came just two weeks after the 2025 results were announced. Reported revenues fell by nearly two points to $6.43 billion. Revenues in the Americas were up over a percent, making up just over half of sales, offset by sluggish trends in Europe, as similar trends were observed in segment margins.

GAAP net losses of $129 million compare to a $106 million loss in 2024, but this comes after steep interest expenses and other losses incurred.

Of interest are the adjusted metrics, with the company reporting an adjusted earnings number of $249 million, compared to GAAP losses of $129 million. The vast majority of the discrepancy comes from $443 million in restructuring, asset impairment, and other charges, offset by various tax benefits.

Adjusted earnings were reported at $1.60 per share, essentially doubling from the year before. Adjusted EBITDA was reported at $1.22 billion, all while net debt of $4.24 billion was flat compared to the year before, for a 3.5 times leverage ratio.

Some Modeling

Ahead of the turmoil in the Middle East, the company guided for 2026 adjusted EBITDA to increase to $1.25-$1.30 billion. This would be just a $30-$80 million improvement from 2025, yet it came after an estimated $150 million step-up in energy costs, with favorable European energy contracts running-off.

These headwinds, are more than offset by the Fit To Win initiative, driving improved performance despite sluggish volume and pricing trends. That would result in adjusted earnings between $1.65-$1.90 per share. Capital spending was seen at $450 million, compared to $432 million in 2025 and $489 million in depreciation & amortization charges reported in 2025.

Of course, by now it is very obvious that the outlook is at risk, at the very best. In between all this, shares have fallen substantially, as the fall from $15 to $10 per share in recent weeks shed about three-quarters of a billion in value. However, with a current $1.5 billion equity valuation, the enterprise valuation has fallen by just a similar amount to $5.7 billion, as that decline is much less pronounced.

The leveraged balance sheet makes those changes in the equity valuation much more pronounced compared to the much more muted changes in the enterprise valuation here, and hence they are not automatically an overreaction to the situation.

Concerns about higher energy prices are real. Ahead of the conflict, energy prices were not really seen as high, as far as I can tell. Moreover, a $150 million headwind in the European operations on a $2.7 billion revenue base is huge already, at 5-6%, essentially cutting segment margins in half. Currently, investors fear the worst, with much more pressure seen here. On an annual basis, I could see incremental headwinds larger than the $150 million energy headwind telegraphed ahead of the conflict. That, of course, assumes the current situation to last for the remainder of the year, as much is unknown.

What is known is that 2026 will be blown from an earnings perspective, given the continuation of the war for more than just a few weeks. While investors can anticipate another year of heavy adjusted earnings adjustments and maybe some positive undertones, actual progress is difficult to see here.

What Now?

In between shares now trading at single-digit adjusted earnings multiples, I note heavy adjustments and the headwinds seen this year. O-I Glass still has lots of leverage and is simply struggling with European operations, all while energy prices impact the business everywhere and discretionary consumer spending is pressured across the globe.

A more definitive solution, such as a breakup of European and US operations, seems more of a necessity to drive long-term value creation for investors, with Europe being a serial underperformer.

Currently, the situation remains highly uncertain, likely disappointing in the near term, as the company is facing sequential revenue declines, negative sales growth, with the company still being in restructuring and cost-cutting mode instead of reporting growth.

While equity looks cheap and might be cheap, leverage is real, adjusted earnings are highly adjusted, no real growth is seen, and despite good intentions, margins remain skinny. This makes me very cautious here, certainly as near-term progress has been more than wiped out given the current events, making me hard-pressed to get involved here despite the value argument.

data.title: IJJ: Hold At Best As Inexpensive Mid-Caps Unlikely To Outmatch The Market
data.description:
In my view, the iShares S&P Mid-Cap 400 Value ETF (IJJ) is likely to underperform both the iShares Core S&P Mid-Cap ETF (IJH) and the iShares S&P 500 ETF (IVV) this year.

As the oil price has plunged and equities have surged amid the ceasefire (even though a two-week one for now), I believe the probability of an escalation scenario, which could have sent the oil prices well above $150 a barrel (pundits even constructed cases for their road to $200) and potentially driven a reversal in Fed policy from interest rate cuts to increases, has declined massively.

The setbacks are certainly possible and perhaps even inevitable (military history of the world has countless examples of ceasefire violations), and bears may even entertain a thought that the reopening of the Strait of Hormuz will be either partial or not happen at all. However, at this juncture, I do not expect these theoretical setbacks to be of a magnitude and duration significant enough to curtail optimism and derail the recovery.

My opinion is that all this creates a buoyant backdrop for growthier large caps (chiefly those in the GARP league I especially appreciate), while the value and low volatility factors have solid chances to miss a lot of upside during the recovery. So vehicles like IJJ that are tilted toward this group of factors are at a disadvantage. And as I also do not see a plausible medium- to long-term bull case for it, I initiate coverage with the Hold rating.

IJJ Strategy And Portfolio

As we know from the iShares website, the lynchpin of IJJ's strategy is the S&P MidCap 400 Value Index, which, as explained in the summary prospectus,

...is a subset of the S&P MidCap 400 ® (the "Parent Index") and consists of those stocks in the Parent Index exhibiting the strongest value characteristics, as determined by S&P Dow Jones Indices LLC (the "Index Provider" or "SPDJI"), a subsidiary of S&P Global, Inc.

While selecting the components, the index provider relies on the trio of metrics:

The value characteristics used by the Index Provider are book value to price ratio, earnings to price ratio and sales to price ratio.

Importantly, in contrast to the S&P MidCap 400® Pure Value Index, which is tracked by the Invesco S&P MidCap 400 Pure Value ETF (RFV), the index is not supposed to offer exposure to the cheapest (i.e., falling into the pure value category) names only. So investors should not be surprised by the fact that 31.7% of IJJ's net assets are allocated to stocks that can also be found in the portfolio of the iShares S&P Mid-Cap 400 Growth ETF (IJK), as per my calculations.

On a side note, IJJ is not the only ETF that tracks this index. There are two others: the Vanguard S&P Mid-Cap 400 Value Index Fund ETF (IVOV) and the State Street SPDR S&P 400 Mid Cap Value ETF (MDYV).

IJJ Portfolio: Factor Mix And Its Implications

As of April 6, there were 301 equities in the IJJ portfolio, with the key ten accounting for just 9.95% of the net assets and US Foods Holding Corp. (USFD) being the primary key holding.

Value and growth factors

Over the years, I have multiple times emphasized that investors favoring inexpensive, misunderstood, and underappreciated equities should always stay vigilant when it comes to ETFs that offer "value strategies." The gist is that methodologies may be too lax when it comes to screening for inexpensiveness, and exposures they produce could be quite distant from what investors may expect.

So the question is, how significant is the presence of value stocks in the IJJ portfolio? I would say it is considerable, though, of course, maximalist value investors have more than one reason for skepticism. Let us look at the facts.

  • About 166 stocks in the IJJ portfolio (58.6% of the net assets) have earnings yields above 4.48%, which is the adjusted weighted average of the S&P 400 ETF portfolio, as per my calculations. And the adjusted WA EY of the IJJ portfolio is 5.52%, 1.2x that of IJH and 1.5x of IVV.
  • Approximately 29.8% of its net assets are allocated to stocks with a B- Quant Value grade or higher. Obviously, this is not a pure-value territory (for instance, as of March 2024, RFV deployed 67.3% to such names). However, it is nonetheless 1.5x larger than IJH's allocation.
  • We also see that IJJ has a smaller weighted average market cap: $9.62 billion vs. $11.597 billion of IJH.

The Price/Sales and EV/EBITDA ratios tell a similar story.

Metrics IJJ IJH IVV
Adjusted P/S 2.91 4.06 8.08
Adjusted EV/EBITDA 10.74 16.23 19.79

Financial data as of April 8. Calculated by the author using data from Seeking Alpha and the ETFs

Delving deeper, we see that IJJ's sector mix is apparently one of the primary contributors to its value characteristics-for instance, compared to IJH, it is solidly overweight in financials (the high-EY sector) and underweight in IT, which tends to be quite pricey.

However, here comes a problem that I have warned about above: obviously, given the essence of the strategy, growthier (and GARP) stocks that I anticipate to outmaneuver value during the recovery are meaningfully underrepresented in this portfolio, as the metrics below illustrate quite vividly.

And on the GARP front, IJJ again offers nothing I can use to construct a thesis upon (the PSG ratio is slightly lower than IJH's, but this is not enough).

Metric IJJ IJH IVV
PEG Ratio (the weighted average P/E divided by the forward EPS growth rate) 1.504 1.455 1.216
Price/Sales-to-Growth Ratio 0.376 0.386 0.539
EV/EBITDA-to-Growth Ratio 1.669 1.476 1.057

Calculated by the author using data from Seeking Alpha and the ETFs

Quality: a few concerns

Quality is a regular point of concern in my ETF analysis pieces that touch upon the small- and mid-cap names. IJJ is, alas, no exception.

More specifically, its weighted average capital efficiency indicators, including the adjusted Return on Equity, Return on Assets, and Return on Total Capital are lower than IJH's (let alone IVV's), while its allocation to stocks with a B- Quant Profitability grade or higher is just 53.29%.

My point here is that maintaining a solid footprint in quality matters regardless of the market regime. And now, as volatility is here to stay, weak quality exposes IJJ to the risk of a much deeper decline compared to IJH and IVV in case the market decides to apply, as I would call it, the Strait of Hormuz discount to equity valuations once again.

A touch of low volatility

Besides, I believe it merits mentioning that IJJ has some exposure to the low volatility factor, as illustrated by its weighted average 24-month beta coefficient (even though the 60-month beta coefficient is at around 1).

Metrics IJJ IJH IVV
24M Beta 0.89 0.98 1.10
60M Beta 1.01 1.06 1.13

Calculated by the author using data from Seeking Alpha and the ETFs

I would say that this is more of a disadvantage at the moment, as low-beta names tend to perform sluggishly during recoveries.

IJJ Performance

IJJ was incepted in July 2000, and its performance over August 2000-March 2026 was nothing short of exemplary, as illustrated by its active return of 1.95% (with IVV used as a benchmark).

What we also see here is that IJJ has captured around 111% of IVV's upside (and 101.29% of IJH's upside), which contradicts my statement that the factor mix exposes IJJ to the risk of missing "a lot of upside during the recovery."

However, the issue with this timeframe is that it includes the early 2000s, a period when IJJ's value characteristics and sector mix somewhat immunized it from the worst during the dot-com bubble burst. In this regard, I believe it makes sense to focus on the recent ten years, April 2016-March 2026.

Here, we see a completely different picture. First, IJJ underperformed both IJH and IVV, delivering a -4.21% active return (with IVV used as a benchmark). Second, its risk-adjusted returns (the Sharpe and Sortino ratios) were also the lowest.

Most importantly, it captured only 97.5% of IVV's upside, and that figure drops to 87.76% over the April 2021-March 2026 period. All in all, my point here is that it is unlikely that IJJ will be able to keep pace with IVV and IJH this year.

Final Thoughts

A few days ago, when the S&P 500 rebounded, I was thinking whether the market truly somehow anticipated a ceasefire or it was a false rebound that lured over-optimistic (or just nonchalant) investors. Now, I can say with relative confidence that the former was the case, even though, again, the possibility of setbacks in the de-escalation process (and some profit-taking translating into occasionally sluggish days for the market) is not to be completely shrugged off and forgotten. In this environment, I favor growth and GARP factors, and IJJ simply does not deliver on them, as it is not supposed to. Additionally, its quality characteristics are far from being to my liking.

Overall, I find IJJ's strategy mostly interesting, and its expense ratio of 18 bps adequate. But, alas, there are no arguments to construct a bullish thesis from at this point. Hence, IJJ is a Hold.

data.title: 6-Month Core PCE Inflation (Still Before Iran War) Jumps By Most Since June 2024. The Fed Needs To Pay Attention
data.description:
“Market-Based Core PCE Price Index,” which excludes the imputed housing components, spiked by the most since February 2023.

The Fed-favored inflation measure, the PCE price index, which includes food and energy, jumped by 0.38% in February from January (+4.6% annualized), the biggest jump in a year, though the energy price spikes from the Iran war hadn’t started yet (blue line).

Year-over-year, the PCE price index rose by 2.8%, roughly the same increase as in the prior three months, and all of them were the biggest increases since the spring of 2024 (red line).

The Fed’s target for the year-over-year measure is 2.0%.

The core PCE price index jumped by 0.37% in February from January, or +4.5% annualized, the third month in a row in the 4%+ annualized range. This pushed up the 6-month core PCE price index to +3.4% annualized, the worst since June 2024. The six-month index shows the recent trend, and that trend has been going in the wrong direction (red line):

The year-over-year core PCE price index (not shown in the chart) rose by 3.0%, a slight deceleration from the prior month (+3.1%). The Fed’s target for this measure is 2.0%.

The housing index, including the imputed housing components, continues to hold down the overall PCE price index, the core PCE price index, and the core services PCE price index.

In February, the housing PCE price index rose by only 0.20% (+2.45% annualized, blue in the chart below). Year-over-year, it rose by 3.1% (red). A major component in the index is “imputed,” so it is not based on price data but on what homeowners think their homes would rent for.

The declining imputed components take the place of actual inflation that homeowners face with surging homeowner’s insurance, property taxes, HOA fees, and repairs and maintenance.

The imputed data is from the BLS, what in CPI is called “owners' equivalent of rent,” which was heavily doctored last fall (explained in detail here), and is visible in the chart below in the outlier three months circled in yellow.

Those three months will continue to push down the year-over-year comparisons until they begin to fall out of the 12-month time frame in September.

But the “market-based core PCE price index,” which excludes all imputed components, spiked by the most since February 2023, by 0.39% (+4.8% annualized). Year-over-year, it rose by 2.9%, the worst since January 2024:

Durable goods prices spiked month-to-month by 1.0% (+13.4% annualized), but not because of motor vehicles, the biggest component, where prices were unchanged month-to-month and nearly unchanged year-over-year; and not because of furnishings and durable household equipment and appliances, which ticked up by 0.3% for the month; but because of huge massive month-to-month spikes related to gold, guns & ammo, and computer software (and I’ll explain how computer software ends up in durable goods):

  • “Jewelry”: +7.0% month to month (+125% annualized), +10.8% year-over-year, as part of the surge of gold prices through January that made it into jewelry retail prices with a lag.
  • “Sporting equipment, supplies, guns, and ammunition”: +1.7% month-to-month (+22% annualized), second big month-to-month spike in a row, which flipped the year-over-year decline through December to an increase of +3.7% year-over-year. This category does not include bicycles, etc., which are in a different category, “sports and recreational vehicles,” where prices dipped in February.
  • “Computer software and accessories”: +6.5% month-to-month (+113% annualized), third big month-to-month increase in a row; +8.0% year-over-year. Software is not tariffed. It’s under durable goods because it’s part of “information processing equipment,” which includes “personal computers & tablets,” which are tariffed, but were unchanged month-to-month (+0%) and barely up year-over-year (+0.9%).

Energy prices jumped in February, before the Iran war, by 0.81% from January (+10.2% annualized), but were still unchanged from a year ago. The energy price spike in March will add to the inflation data for March.

Food prices rose by 0.27% in February from January (+3.3% annualized, blue line). Year-over-year, they rose by 2.3%. The trend has been accelerating since mid-2024.

Part of it has been driven by the four-year-long huge price increases of beef and veal (+14% over the latest 12 months) and coffee (+12% over the latest 12 months). Some other food prices, such as eggs, have fallen from their prior spikes but remain much higher than before.

Inflation isn’t going away; on the contrary. There is a lot of stimulus in the economy: Government deficit spending, tax cuts for companies and individuals, bigger tax refunds to consumers starting in March, massive corporate investments in anything related to AI, too-low interest rates, and still too narrow spreads. Inflation feeds on these conditions.

The housing index and especially the declining imputed measures that replace the soaring actual inflation homeowners face are covering up the extent of the inflationary pressures in the economy.

The Fed needs to pay attention to inflation, and it has to some extent, and it keeps mentioning it as a risk to the upside, and it has put rate cuts on hold because of it.

And in case you missed it: New inflation pressures suddenly building up in the pipeline are concerning – used vehicle wholesale prices jumped. That’s how it started in 2020 when broad inflation took off

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Powell Industries: Stock Split Brings Noise, Hold Until Further Growth Signs
data.description:
Powell Industries (POWL) is a specialized provider of custom-engineered electrical infrastructure systems used to control, distribute, and manage power in industrial environments. The company serves end markets including utilities, oil & gas, transportation, and the ever-growing data center market. They are a viable supplier to large-scale capital projects tied to energy infrastructure and separate themselves from competitors by their highly engineered, project-specific solutions.

The stock is up 90% YTD and 84% since I gave them a buy rating right before their Q4 2025 earnings last November. At the time I had anticipated exceptional earnings that did not disappoint, provoking a bull run that has moved the share price from $95 to now well over $200. Earnings growth, industry tailwinds on high demand, and discounted valuation led to this nice run.

The company recently completed a 3-for-1 stock split, a move aimed at improving share accessibility and trading liquidity following its strong share price appreciation. The stock has gained 20% in less than a week since the split. Valuation has risen and leads me to believe that growth needs to scale more and quickly to catch up to what the share price has done. I think growth on continued backlog and order strength will extend the earnings trend, but I think I would need to see multiple quarters of huge earnings expansion before another buy rating. Margins remain high, and the stock split could allow for increased demand at a lower share price, but I have POWL as a hold right now.

The Stock Split Effect

The three-for-one stock split began at market open on April 6, and the company said they expect this will increase the number of shares of their outstanding common stock from 12.1 million shares to about 36.4 million shares. CEO Brett Cope said this is a reflection of strong performance and confidence in the future outlook.

The stock split adjusts accordingly and does not affect their valuation, which has recently risen. A key piece to the split is that it increases liquidity and can attract more investors, often institutional investors, which may support the stock price in the short term. At a lower price, more money could flow into the stock, increasing perception and demand for the stock. However, this doesn’t change the company’s fundamentals, so I do think it’s more important to look at this from a wider lens and not pay as much attention to what the stock does in the next few weeks. On POWL’s behalf, I do think their fundamentals are very much intact, but I don’t know that the current or expected growth can keep pace with how high the stock has climbed.

Valuation and Risk

I don’t see a whole lot of risk for POWL based on strong internal and industry trends. The most obvious risk is the rise in valuation over the last 4-5 months, and it is the big reason why I am moving them to a hold now. PE has doubled from 20x to 41x since November. There have been solid earnings, including a record high adjusted EPS of $1.41 in Q4 25, followed by $1.13 in Q1 26, which was still a 19% increase YoY.

Adjusted EPS over the last 4 quarters is up just 17% from the prior 4 quarters. This is solid growth but nothing to write home about, and based on the massive price return, investors should expect more. Growth does need to continue and even ramp up, or investors will see the stock soon as overpriced. Price to book is 3x the sector median, and even with strong growth expected, the stock boasts an inflated PEG of 2.32, much higher than the industry.

Growth and Profits

EPS has grown by 67% compounded on an annual basis over the last 5 years. This is more the type of growth I’m talking about when describing they need more to keep up with the share price. The advancements have continued but tapered off a little over the last year. Revenue growth has averaged close to 18% on an annual basis, which is solid, and shows that profitability has been increasing on a per share basis.

Net income margin has reached 16.82%, almost triple the sector median, and operating margin is at an all-time high of 20%. POWL offers custom-based solutions, which has become more popular by demand over standardized electrical equipment. This niche allows for greater margins and operating leverage over peers as their exposure to data centers and large utility projects keep gaining.

Backlog & Demand

POWL has a record backlog near $1.6 billion. Q1 26 is off to a solid start, with the help of a $100 million LNG contract and a $75 million data center project. But again, with my slight concern of a slowdown in growth, you can see that the real hike in backlog took place in 2023, and it has tapered since. I think we could see periods of further expansion again, though, as management notes they are seeing healthy bookings even into the second half of 2027.

Orders, which reflect the demand POWL is seeing but is yet to secure, also escalated quickly a few years back and have actually dropped over the last two years. Q1 2026 has had a very robust start on the order front; it could be an indicator of getting back on track with order progress, which I do anticipate. Electrical infrastructure for AI data centers is in early stages, and POWL has increased its presence as a global leader here.

Q2 Results Predictions

POWL will report Q2 earnings in early May, with EPS estimates around $1.29-$1.32 at the moment. Revisions have shifted slightly upward recently as analysts are bullish on an earnings beat. Q2 2025 was $1.27, so even if results come in near projections, they are a bit higher; the YoY growth is still lower than what the stock rise has implied. I do think they beat earnings and might come in even closer to $1.40, as Q1 was strong for backlog and orders. Management is likely to spend some good time on the call discussing the noise and excitement around the stock split. I wouldn't be shocked to see the stock continue its run through earnings, especially if they do beat. But, we need to look at the bigger picture here and see that the growth in sales and EPS are not keeping up with the share price, and a pullback seems almost imminent to me.

Conclusion

POWL has seen huge price gains over the last year or so, with an even greater uptick in the last week since the 3-for-1 stock split. This shows management confidence and will increase liquidity and possibly gain investor attention over the near term. I do think that the stock's valuation has become too inflated, based on the recent moderate earnings and backlog growth.

POWL has gained in revenue across their U.S. and International segments and operates manufacturing sites and sales offices across the world. For holders of this stock, I definitely do not warrant a sell, but I do think the raised value could catch up with them unless they see a couple of huge earnings beats. I like POWL, but until growth ramps higher, I have this stock as a hold.

data.title: Hormel Foods: The Undervalued Dividend King Adapting To Evolving Consumer Trends
data.description:
Introduction

Back when I first covered Hormel Foods Corporation (HRL), I called them a “Dividend King That's Too Cheap To Ignore At Decade Lows,” highlighting their solid dividend yield and strong fundamentals, with a portfolio that can adapt to some of the ongoing trends and risks in the industry, potentially allowing them to continue their slow and steady growth trajectory.

With the stock now down about 12.5% since then, the valuation seems to take into account significant levels of risk, while the company remains in a healthy financial position and should be able to keep paying and increasing their dividend in the long run.

Business As Usual, So Far

Despite the ongoing macro pressure, HRL’s latest report was fine overall, with a slight beat on EPS and a small miss on the market’s revenue estimates, with a 1.6% increase in net sales, marked by a 7.4% increase in foodservice and 7.7% internationally against a 1.8% drop in retail, while the free cash flow reached a solid $280.22 million.

For the rest of FY26, HRL continues to expect net sales of $12.2 billion to $12.5 billion, a 1% to 4% increase in organic net sales growth, and $1.43 to $1.51 in Adj. Diluted EPS, while the adjusted operating income would grow by a strong 4% to 10% to $1.06 billion to $1.12 billion while recently selling their whole-bird turkey business to further streamline operations and reduce their exposure to commodities.

The CAPEX is also still expected to reach $260 million to $290 million in FY26, below last year's $310.9 million, potentially boosting their FCF alongside their inventory normalization following the build-up in 2025.

Meanwhile, the company's long-term goals continue to target 2% to 3% organic net sales growth and 5% to 7% operating income growth, with a specific focus on their protein-centric portfolio.

Financially, based on HRL’s latest report, we continue to see a very solid position, with the current assets covering their current liabilities several times and a good chunk of their long-term debt, staying relatively stable throughout the quarter.

HRL is a Dividend King with 6 decades of consecutive annual increases, with a yield that's now standing at about 5.45%, representing a bit over $640 million per year following the previous 0.9% increase from November 2025.

Macro Uncertainty Gets Worse

On top of the consumer weakness that caused trade-downs/trade-offs mentioned before, which so far had significant effects on the retail and foodservice industries, we saw significantly more pressure in the months following the previous article.

The ongoing conflict in Iran can push this environment into a much tougher spot even if the conflict were to end immediately - which is already questionable a day after the ceasefire was announced. That’s because of the inflationary shock it can cause, which in turn can affect the Fed’s (and other central banks’) next moves, with the market’s hopes of a rate cut fading as this conflict takes its toll on the region’s energy capabilities.

On top of these, we saw a recent report from Deutsche Bank analyst Steve Powers and his team highlighting the factors I also mentioned before that challenge the idea of a “defensive play” for the CPG industry, with factors such as GLP-1 risks, aging demographics, deepening K-shaped economic discrepancy, and especially private labels pressuring the industry in the near- and long-term.

In my opinion, competition and private labels are by far the biggest issue, as retailers are thus able to gain significant market share without even having to invest billions in marketing like legacy brands did. Given the growth of the “K-shaped economy” and pressure especially on the lower-income households, private labels can gain significant ground and very important data, allowing them to tailor their offer for and potentially release better value, higher price points, etc., depending on the consumer environment.

Regarding the GLP-1/MAHA/health consciousness point made before, we have a new study from Morgan Stanley, which now expects GLP-1 medication usage to reach ~15% of the US population by 2035, which further highlights my previous points that the impact of this factor is not going to be as significant as the market fears, especially given HRL’s products.

Still, this can be one factor out of many that can pressure the industry (alongside a fight against ultra-processed food), but I believe it’s important to note that these companies don’t act in a void, and there are several steps they can take to adapt - with their protein focus already being announced - and out of all the major food producers, HRL is among the best-prepared for several consumer trends, with the CEO highlighting:

When we look beyond segment performance, the larger trends in consumer behavior and protein demand strongly affirm both where we stand today and where we're going. I spoke to this extensively at the CAGNY conference last week. Our path forward continues to start with the consumer, and it's anchored in the unique position we've built in the protein space over the years. As we highlighted at CAGNY, protein isn't a passing trend. It's an enduring long-term movement. As we follow the consumer and sharpen our focus on protein-led growth, our strategy must also evolve.

Risks Reiterated

Although we’re talking about a solid, healthy company that’s among the best-prepared in terms of future trends among larger peers, they and their valuation still depend on the economy, and the Iran-linked inflationary pressure can be very significant for it.

Meanwhile, competitive pressure, especially from private labels, is a major long-term threat, as even though they have solid and popular brands, this environment pushed a lot of consumers into trying and seeing the quality of private label brands, which, if smart, can capture a potential recovery in the long term.

Valuation

For HRL’s DCF to Equity valuation, assuming the inventory normalization and CAPEX decrease mentioned previously, I’ll continue to use $750 million in free cash flow as the base in 2026 (which should be quite conservative on purpose), assuming no change in net borrowings given their solid balance sheet that doesn’t need it.

I’ll assume this can grow at a 5% CAGR over the first 5-year period - the lower end of their long-term operating income growth target - thanks to things such as their international expansion, portfolio optimization, protein demand, and other macro improvements, followed by a 3.5% CAGR for the subsequent 5 years and a 2.5% terminal growth rate, with a discount rate of 8.5% this time to take into account the higher level of risk and demand a better return, with 550.71 million shares outstanding as of their Q1’26 report.

This way, we get to an equity value of $14.06 billion, meaning an intrinsic value of $25.53 per share, which is quite well above the current $21.54 at the moment of writing this, which can signal that the market is even more conservative, pricing in significant levels of risk already.

Conclusions

HRL remains a Buy, backed by their strong financial position, attractive dividend, and overall solid portfolio that can adapt to long-term trends and changes in consumer behavior.

Of course, there are risks to keep in mind, especially given the industry and the rise of private labels, but the company’s international/food service expansion is also an advantage that can allow them to develop around it for a long time while rewarding shareholders a solid dividend yield.

data.title: Full Employment May Still Signal Stagnation In Labor
data.description:
By Antón Chamberlin

Low layoffs and low hiring have created a fragile equilibrium. The underlying picture points to drift, not true growth.

Federal Reserve Chair Jerome Powell recently emphasized that the US economy remains resilient, in large part due to a labor market holding steady, despite growing uncertainty. The direct words Powell used to describe the current situation were a kind of “zero employment growth equilibrium.”

There is limited hiring, yes, but also limited layoffs. When combined with persistently low jobless claims, policymakers seem to be of the opinion that conditions remain stable, even strong, in toto. On paper, we are very near full employment. Unfortunately, this is only part of the story. Upon digging, the labor market shows signs of strain.

Hiring has markedly slowed, and recent data and surveys point to noticeably weaker hiring conditions compared with the last decade. Some reports show outright job losses along with rising unemployment, challenging the notion of continued strength.

Yet layoffs remain subdued, and even jobless claims are staying low, signaling that firms are holding onto their workers. This “low-hire, low-fire” labor market appears stable, but it lacks forward momentum. What we appear to have is an economy that is neither collapsing nor improving - drifting, not growing.

And so we reach this strange purported equilibrium. Labor markets are not collapsing, but they are not advancing with any force, either. This stability is stagnant.

Powell did add that this stagnation “does have a feel of downside risk, and it’s not kind of a really comfortable balance,” but alarm bells are not ringing in DC yet. When looking at employment data, this strange picture emerges. Here, then, we see the limits of employment data, like the concept of full employment.

Investopedia explains full employment like this: “Full employment exists when all willing and available skilled and unskilled labor is being used.” This is not to say that the unemployment rate is truly zero percent, but it is meant as a theoretical goal.

Some people will be willfully unemployed for various reasons. A laid-off mechanical engineer will take some time to look for new jobs in his field before considering other lines of work. The nurse who quits due to understaffing will not necessarily jump into the same situation at a different hospital, but might hold out for a better work environment.

Excluding these individuals, the rate should reach zero percent. Often, full employment is defined as an unemployment rate sitting between four and six percent. The US unemployment rate for February was 4.4 percent, falling within the full employment range. Here we see one source of “optimism” for Washington.

As is often the case, this indicator cannot capture the uneven, localized, and shifting nature of real work. In practice, the labor market is a process that cannot be captured in a single snapshot. Workers search, firms adjust, industries expand and contract, and skill levels increase and decrease.

At any given moment, some sectors are growing while others decline. The same applies to regions. These details are often smoothed over by aggregates. This means that telling us where the average stands does not indicate how the system itself functions on the ground. A stable national picture can hide underlying volatility. Balance in the aggregate may in reality be nothing but a series of offsetting imbalances.

For example, consider underemployment. A man desiring full-time work settling for part-time hours is still employed. A college graduate working retail despite a degree suited for investment analysis is still technically employed, despite underutilization of his skillset. These distinctions matter for economic well-being, despite hardly registering in headline statistics.

Even the data themselves can be misleading. Employment figures are often revised, sometimes drastically. Many times these revisions show weaker job creation than originally reported, changing the narrative from strength to concern.

Recent revisions from the Bureau of Labor Statistics have erased hundreds of thousands of previously reported jobs - in some cases, completely flipping growth months into contraction months. None of this is helpful, nor is it a good reason for optimism.

Likewise, structural forces reshape labor demand. Technological changes and the ability to switch jobs affect the market in ways not so quickly detectable. The goal is not mere employment but good matches between employers and employees to maximize value creation.

People remaining in a job because it is increasingly difficult to switch does not signal health. When necessary reallocation is not happening, we lose out on potential growth and rising efficiency, even if unemployment figures remain stable.

When just looking at unemployment figures, it is easy for a policymaker to confuse full capacity with true economic vitality. This misreading has its consequences. Policymakers, equipped with the specious belief that they can beneficently direct the economy, might focus on restricting demand so as to avoid inflation.

Or they might defer to current policies that are actually weakening the economy, but only in a less obvious way (or in such a way as to only become manifest months later in a “revision”).

With all this in mind, we must be careful how we choose to measure economic health. All national statistics provide somewhat useful summaries but cannot replace dispersed, local knowledge embedded in actual market activities.

The economy does not employ “labor” in the abstract, but rather it employs specific people, with specific skills, in specific places, at specific times, for specific reasons. When this complexity gets reduced to a single number like the unemployment rate, we lose clarity for the sake of a simplistic metric.

An economy can appear to be fully employed on paper while being less dynamic, less accessible, and less resilient in practice. Headlines might read “strong” or “stable,” but the economy is more than aggregate figures. It is a web of individual choices and adjustments, and those adjustments are real and important but not fully captured in a headline number.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.
